topic: Batch post-hoc rebuttal sentences appended to 62 WINs (T/D/V/O categories)
overall_assessment: ACCEPT WITH MINOR MODIFICATIONS. The batch is well-executed: all 62 WINs correctly received their category-appropriate template, the 6 already-addressed WINs were correctly excluded, the 2 post_hoc=false WINs (031, 044) were correctly skipped, no duplicate sentences exist, all anchor links (#posthoc-standard, dome author URL) are well-formed and resolve correctly, and the 'Independent of the scientific critique above' transition reads smoothly across all sampled WINs. The category assignments (T/D/V/O) are largely accurate. Two issues warrant attention: (1) the V template is semantically mismatched for Unfalsifiable/theological WINs, and (2) the batch inherits Section 3.1b's unresolved OpenTimestamps gap.

WIN-001_D.notes: Tesla 11.78 Hz resonance. The confirming frequency IS from a field-specific published source (Colorado Springs notes, telluric measurements). D template fits perfectly. The dome claimed this as PROS-001 — the D template's 'publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit' language is exactly right.

WIN-016_T.notes: Stellar aberration / refractive model. The standard values for aberration (20.5 arcsec) ARE textbook physics. T template's 'undergraduate references' language is accurate.

WIN-012_V.notes: κ = 1.672 coupling constant. This IS a dome-derived/speculative value — the dome extracted it from one geomagnetic event and claimed it as a universal constant. V template's 'derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters' fits. The circularity argument (parameters fit to globe data) is valid here.

WIN-038_T.notes: Schumann 7.83 Hz. Published by Schumann 1952. Quintessential textbook value. T template is exactly right.

WIN-067_D.notes: Antarctic gravity hole. The Antarctic gravity anomaly data IS from published satellite surveys (GRACE, Pappa et al. 2019). D template fits.

tidal_wins_045_046_049_050_051.notes: Tidal constituent periods (M2, S2, K1, O1, N2) are in every physical oceanography textbook. T category is correct.

unfalsifiable_wins_023_032_034.notes: These are theological/unfalsifiable claims, not numerical dome-geometry derivations. The V template says 'The numerical value is one the dome framework derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters' — but WIN-034 (copper firmament) has no numerical value, and WIN-032 (New Jerusalem axis) is a biblical assertion. The 'symbolic parameters' reading is technically defensible but the 'numerical value' opener is factually wrong for these WINs. A dome defender could accurately say 'you applied a circularity-of-derivation rebuttal to a claim about what the firmament is made of — there IS no derivation to be circular.' The harm is low but the precision is off.

obscure_wins_003_011_015.notes: WIN-003 (King's Chamber 10th harmonic), WIN-011 (Mohe 1997 gravity anomaly), WIN-015 (Meyl scalar Faraday) — these are genuinely obscure references that warrant the verdict-review flag. The O template correctly flags them rather than asserting a final rebuttal.

batch_integrity_checks.all_transitions_start_with: Independent of the scientific critique above

holes_found.description: V-template mismatch for Unfalsifiable/theological WINs (023, 032, 034). The V template opens with 'The numerical value is one the dome framework derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters' — but WIN-034 (copper firmament) and WIN-032 (New Jerusalem axis) have no numerical values and no derivations. The template's circularity argument ('because the dome parameters were themselves fit to globe data') does not apply to theological claims. A dome defender could correctly say: 'You told me my claim about the firmament material fails because my parameters are fit to globe data. But my claim comes from Job 37:18 and Exodus 38:8, not from fitting curves to IGRF data. You applied the wrong rebuttal.'
holes_found.recommendation: Create a fifth template category (U for Unfalsifiable) for WINs where the claim is theological/non-empirical: 'This claim is additionally a post-hoc observation rather than a prospective prediction: it restates an ancient textual assertion that cannot be tested against physical measurement, so the timing distinction between prediction and retrodiction is moot. The dome monitoring scripts contain no check for this WIN because there is no observable to check.' Apply to WIN-023, WIN-032, WIN-034. Alternatively, if a fifth template is deemed too much infrastructure, simply replace 'The numerical value' with 'This claim' in the V template, which makes it fit both dome-derived numbers and theological assertions. The second option is a smaller change.

holes_found.description: The batch inherits Section 3.1b's OpenTimestamps gap. Every appended sentence says the dome author's definition is 'unverifiable' because 'the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line.' But 5 of the dome's 9 self-declared PROSPECTIVE WINs (WIN-035, 036, 037, 038, 039) are 'weekly confirmations' where the dome claims the prediction was registered with a specific date before the confirming data pull. If those WINs have OpenTimestamps anchoring (the dome claims they do), the D-template sentence 'the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history' is refuted by the dome's own evidence — the ordering CAN be checked via the .ots proof file. This does not mean the WINs are prospective (they still fail criterion (b) on provable unawareness), but the specific sub-argument about temporal ordering being uncheckable is wrong for OTS-anchored WINs.
holes_found.recommendation: Wait for the Section 3.1b OpenTimestamps paragraph to be written (per the SEC-3.1b.c2 review recommendations). Once that paragraph exists and addresses 'blockchain proves timing but not unawareness,' the per-WIN D-template sentence will be contextually correct because the reader can follow the anchor to the full explanation. In the interim, this is a known gap, not a blocker — the post-hoc tag still holds on the primary ground (known public dataset, no provable unawareness).

holes_found.description: The D-template sentence parenthetical '(IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.)' is a generic list that doesn't match every D-category WIN. WIN-047 (low-z Hubble Law) confirmed against galaxy redshift surveys, not IGRF. WIN-048 (CMB Axis of Evil) confirmed against WMAP/Planck data, not NASA station archives. The parenthetical doesn't claim to be exhaustive ('etc.'), so it's not factually wrong, but a dome defender reviewing multiple WINs would notice the list doesn't match the specific WIN's data source.
holes_found.recommendation: Accept as-is. The 'etc.' makes it non-exhaustive. Per-WIN source customization of 62 sentences would be impractical and the parenthetical serves its purpose (illustrating what 'field-specific published dataset' means). This is cosmetic, not substantive.

holes_found.description: All 62 appended sentences begin with 'Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test.' The word 'also' implies a secondary/supplementary argument. For some WINs where the primary rebuttal is weak (e.g., borderline 'Misleading' verdicts), the post-hoc argument may actually be the STRONGER of the two rebuttals. The 'also' undersells it. However, the 'Independent of' framing is correct — the post-hoc argument IS logically independent — so 'also' is accurate even if it reads as subordinate.
holes_found.recommendation: Accept as-is. Consistency across 62 WINs is more important than per-WIN tuning of the rhetorical weight.

stronger_arguments.description: The batch sentences are defensive — they explain why each WIN fails our post-hoc criteria. A stronger version would also note what a genuine prospective prediction from the dome model would LOOK LIKE: a specific numerical value, derived from dome geometry, for a measurement that has not yet been taken, published in a timestamped venue before the measurement date. The August 2026 eclipse station-amplitude predictions (E001-E009) are the only current candidates. Noting this in the T or D templates would preempt the 'you're just rejecting everything' criticism.

kernel_of_truth.description: The batch execution is skilled infrastructure work. Standardizing the post-hoc rebuttal across 62 WINs with category-sensitive templates is exactly the right approach — it ensures consistency of argument while allowing the specific evidence class (textbook, dataset, dome-derived, obscure) to drive the per-WIN language. The T/D/V/O categorization is a genuine analytical contribution that could stand on its own as a classification result.
kernel_of_truth.why_modifications_needed: The V-template mismatch for theological WINs is a precision error that a careful reader will notice. The OpenTimestamps inheritance is a known gap tracked at the section level. Neither threatens the batch's overall validity.

modifications_required: Fix V-template opener for Unfalsifiable WINs (023, 032, 034): replace 'The numerical value is one the dome framework derives' with a broader phrasing that covers non-numerical theological claims. Simplest fix: 'This claim is one the dome framework bases on its own geometric, symbolic, or theological sources'
No other changes needed — the batch is well-executed and the remaining issues are either inherited from Section 3.1b (will be fixed there) or cosmetic (accept as-is)

text_fingerprint.batch_scope: 62 WINs patched with category-sensitive post-hoc sentences

citations_verified: #posthoc-standard anchor — exists in docs/index.html (1 occurrence, correct location)
https://john09289.github.io/predictions — dome author link, correct URL per raw-text archives
Part 2b cross-reference in D template — anchor #part2b exists
citations_unchecked: Kill-Shot #1 reference in V template — not verified which specific kill-shot this points to; the generated HTML should resolve it via the kill-shots section

deeper_analysis:
The EXP-054 batch is a well-engineered piece of review infrastructure. The analyst classified 68 post-hoc WINs into four evidence-class categories, wrote templates that address the specific argumentative weakness of each class, and applied them consistently with correct exclusion logic for already-addressed and not-post-hoc WINs. The batch also correctly handles the transition — 'Independent of the scientific critique above' is a clean clause-break that separates the domain-specific rebuttal from the methodological post-hoc argument.

The V-template mismatch for theological WINs is the only substantive issue. It's a case where the categorization system (T/D/V/O) was designed for empirical claims and then applied to non-empirical ones. The fix is small — a word change in 3 WINs — but the principle matters: we cannot apply a circularity-of-derivation argument to claims that are not derived from anything. Theological claims fail the post-hoc test for a DIFFERENT reason than empirical claims: there is no observation to retrodict from, and no measurement to timestamp. The U-template or the broadened V-template opener would capture this.

On the other side of the argument (per user preference): a dome defender reviewing the batch would focus on the 9 PROSPECTIVE WINs (004, 007, 010, 024, 035-039) and argue that our D-template sentence — 'the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history' — is directly contradicted by OpenTimestamps proof files that DO allow checking. This is a fair criticism that the batch cannot resolve on its own; it depends on Section 3.1b being patched to address OpenTimestamps. Once that patch lands, the per-WIN sentences gain their missing context. Until then, the batch has a known soft spot that a sophisticated dome defender can exploit.
